coredata.dc:title: Is silicon worth it? Modelling degradation in composite silicon–graphite lithium-ion battery electrodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Bonkile, Mayur P.
Jiang, Yang
Kirkaldy, Niall
Sulzer, Valentin
Timms, Robert
Wang, Huizhi
Offer, Gregory
Wu, Billy
coredata.dc:description: The addition of silicon into graphite lithium-ion battery anodes has the potential to increase cell energy density. However, understanding the complex degradation behaviour in these composite systems remains a research challenge. Here, we developed a coupled electrochemical–mechanical model of a composite silicon/graphite electrode, including stress-driven crack formation and solid electrolyte interphase layer growth for each material, validated with experimental degradation data from an LG M50T cell. The model reveals self-limiting loss of silicon due to decreasing stress in the silicon as the silicon activity shifts to a lower state-of-charge. Higher C-rates can lead to lower degradation due to lower phase utilisation as voltage cut-offs are reached earlier. Increasing silicon content can reduce the stress in the silicon by distributing reaction current density over more material. Using this model, we explored whether the extra capacity from silicon is generally ‘worth’ the faster degradation compared to graphite-only electrodes. The model shows if you use the silicon, you lose it, as the higher initial capacity is rapidly lost with regular high depth-of-discharge events. However, silicon does have value if it enables full graphite utilisation without range anxiety; if high depth-of-discharge events are minimised then graphite’s superior longevity can be utilised while exploiting silicon’s high specific capacity. The model is integrated into PyBaMM (an open-source physics-based modelling platform); providing the research community and industry with the capability to reproduce our results and further explore the dynamic lifetime behaviour of composite electrodes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite anodes
Silicon/graphite
Lithium-ion battery
Degradation mode analysis
Physics-based model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Influence of C-rate and silicon content

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Overview of the research work done in this paper.
Degradation sub-model interaction in PyBaMM framework.

Degradation mode analysis for cell cycled at different SoC cycling ranges. Solid lines represent the simulation results, with shaded regions corresponding to the experiment results: (a) Loss of active material of the anode at 0-100% SoC range, (b) loss of active material of the silicon at 0-100% SoC range, and (c) loss of active material of the graphite at 0-100% SoC range as a function of charge throughput, (d) Loss of active material of the anode at 0-30% SoC range, (e) loss of active material of the silicon at 0-30% SoC range, and (f) loss of active material of the graphite at 0-30% SoC range as a function of charge throughput.

Evolution of stress profiles and dynamic stress changes for cell cycled at different SoC cycling ranges: The effect of degradation on (a) the average volumetric interfacial current density at 0-100% SoC range, stress evolution in (b) silicon and (c) graphite at 0-100% SoC range, (d) the average volumetric interfacial current density at 0-30% SoC range, and stress evolution in (b) silicon and (c) graphite at 0-30% SoC range.

Cell cycled at 0%–100% SoC range: The stress distribution and evolutions at BoL and EoL under different C-rates of discharging: (a) graphite and (b) silicon phase. The impact of Si content variation on (c) cell discharge capacity, (d) averaged interfacial current density and (e) influence of C-rates and Si content on LAM
S i
and LAM
G r
.

Cell cycled at 0%–30% SoC range: The stress distribution and evolutions at BoL and EoL under different C-rates of discharging: (a) graphite and (b) silicon phase and (c) LAM
S i
and LAM
G r
(10 wt% Si). The impact of Si content variation on averaged interfacial current density during discharge in (d) graphite, (e) silicon phase and (e) influence of Si content on the active material fraction of graphite and silicon at 1C discharge.

Capacity loss trends for different composite cell designs operated over different SoC ranges and usage profiles, compared to an equivalent graphite-only cell. All cells are cycled with C-rates of 0.3C and 1C for charge and discharge, respectively.

head.ce:title: Is silicon worth it? Modelling degradation in composite silicon–graphite lithium-ion battery electrodes

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dyson School of Design Engineering, Imperial College London

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The addition of silicon into graphite lithium-ion battery anodes has the potential to increase cell energy density. However, understanding the complex degradation behaviour in these composite systems remains a research challenge. Here, we developed a coupled electrochemical–mechanical model of a composite silicon/graphite electrode, including stress-driven crack formation and solid electrolyte interphase layer growth for each material, validated with experimental degradation data from an LG M50T cell. The model reveals self-limiting loss of silicon due to decreasing stress in the silicon as the silicon activity shifts to a lower state-of-charge. Higher C-rates can lead to lower degradation due to lower phase utilisation as voltage cut-offs are reached earlier. Increasing silicon content can reduce the stress in the silicon by distributing reaction current density over more material. Using this model, we explored whether the extra capacity from silicon is generally ‘worth’ the faster degradation compared to graphite-only electrodes. The model shows if you use the silicon, you lose it, as the higher initial capacity is rapidly lost with regular high depth-of-discharge events. However, silicon does have value if it enables full graphite utilisation without range anxiety; if high depth-of-discharge events are minimised then graphite’s superior longevity can be utilised while exploiting silicon’s high specific capacity. The model is integrated into PyBaMM (an open-source physics-based modelling platform); providing the research community and industry with the capability to reproduce our results and further explore the dynamic lifetime behaviour of composite electrodes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

Number of cracks per unit area of the particle [m−2]
Concentration of lithium in phase k [molm−3]

Lithium diffusion coefficient of phase k [m
2
s−1]

Reaction rate in phase k [Am
2 . 5
mol
− 1 . 5
]
Number of cathode and anode layers
Particle radius of phase k [ μ m]

SEI partial molar volume,
m
3
mol
− 1

ce:section.ce:para: However, various degradation mechanisms persist, including: the formation and growth of the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI), transition metal dissolution which augments SEI layer growth, crack formation and propagation, and the loss of active material (LAM) [7,8]. During cycling, concentration gradients within the active material particles arise due to limited solid-phase lithium-ion mobility, leading to the development of diffusion-induced stress and strain profiles [9,10]. This stress is a significant driving force for particle cracking, crack propagation, and island formation; contributing to irreversible battery capacity and power loss [11].

In recent years, various models have been developed to provide a deeper understanding of the underlying battery dynamics. For instance, Albertus et al. [12] simulated the effects of multi-active material electrodes with different particle sizes, film resistances, and particle-conductive matrix contact resistances. In the realm of degradation modelling, Chen et al. [13] coupled mechanical and electrochemical physics in a model to study the behaviour of batteries with composite silicon/graphite electrodes. Here, results suggest that the negative electrode experiences asymmetric volume changes and stress evolution between charge and discharge due to the different lithiation states of silicon and graphite. Increasing silicon content can improve capacity but also leads to greater volume expansion during cycling, resulting in increased pressure within the cell and reduced porosity [14]. In other work, Liu et al. [15] showed that silicon content, silicon/graphite particle ratio, and battery size significantly impacts capacity, battery deformation, and charging time. One of the reasons for capacity fade is caused by particle cracking, leading to loss of active material, and SEI growth on an increased surface area of the composite electrode upon cycling [16]. However, the model assumes a lumped composite silicon/graphite electrode; which does not enable decoupling of the interactions between the two phases.

Numerous other studies have been published in recent years on composite silicon/graphite battery electrode degradation, yet several research challenges remain. These include, developing holistic understanding and decoupling how multi-physics effects impact composite electrode lifetime. During operation, silicon and graphite particles undergo differing (de)lithiation behaviour, driven by distinct thermodynamic and kinetic properties, with silicon’s reaction current being reduced compared to graphite, particularly at higher C-rates [17]. Mechanical degradation is widely recognised as a crucial factor in the cyclic ageing of these composite electrodes. Diffusion-induced stress has been commonly employed to describe ageing behaviour [18] alongside the SEI growth, which leads to irreversible consumption of lithium-ions [19].

Driven by these challenges, this study seeks to (a) introduce a comprehensive multi-physics battery model that amalgamates electrochemical dynamics, mechanical stresses, and chemo-mechanical capacity degradation; (b) validate the proposed model experimentally; and (c) analyse different operational and design scenarios to investigate the value of silicon in the composite electrodes. We deploy these governing equations in PyBaMM (Python Battery Mathematical Modelling); an open-source battery simulation package written in Python [20], which combines the pseudo-two-dimensional (P2D) model with electrochemical and mechanical degradation physics, resulting in better predictions of dynamic capacity degradation, as highlighted in Fig. 1. This study focuses on the LG M50T battery featuring an NMC811 cathode and a composite silicon/graphite anode. To investigate the electrochemical–mechanical behaviour of the battery, a corresponding P2D model integrating a solvent diffusion-based SEI growth model and LAM model was developed. This involves incorporating SEI formation and growth, particle cracking, diffusion-induced stress, and stress-induced diffusion. Utilising this comprehensive model, cycling-induced ageing phenomena were systematically examined under diverse operating conditions. The model is able to deconvolute the capacity decline caused by these degradation mechanisms under varying Depth-of-Discharge (DoD), C-rates and silicon content. The results obtained from this study contribute to a better understanding of composite electrode degradation and provide valuable insights for developing improved electrodes and designs.

Commercial 21700 cylindrical cells (LG M50T, LG GBM50T2170), were cycled over two different state of charge (SoC) ranges (0%–30% and 0%–100%) at 25 °C with C-rates of 0.3C and 1C for charge and discharge, respectively. Cells cycled in the 0%–30% SoC range were discharged to the lower voltage cut-off of 2.5 V and charged by passing 1.5 Ah (equal to 0.3 × the 5 Ah nominal capacity). Cells cycled over the 0%–100% SoC range were discharged to 2.5 V and charged to 4.2 V, with a constant voltage hold at 4.2 V until the current dropped to 0.05 A. Two cells were tested for the 0%–100% SoC range and three cells for the 0%–30% range. All cells were thermally managed by base-cooling, with a constant boundary condition of 25 °C on the base of the cell and thermal insulation on the cylindrical surface. Electrical connections for the positive and negative terminals were both via the top of the cell. Electrochemical cycling was performed using a Biologic BCS-815 battery cycler and using the accompanying BT-Lab software. A reference performance test was periodically performed to measure the extent of degradation; this included a C/10 (dis)charge cycle, which was used to estimate degradation modes of loss of lithium inventory and loss of active material using the OCV-fitting method [21,22]. This allowed the loss of each active material to be tracked in-situ as the cells aged. The detailed testing procedure can be found in [22]. The accompanying data can be found in [23].

The literature contains various lithium-ion battery models, ranging from simple empirical ones [24,25] to more complex physics-based models [26,27]. Considering the complex capacity fade dynamics in composite electrodes, empirical models are not well suited for accurately describing underlying degradation modes. Physics-based battery models, on the other-hand, are able to provide physical insights into degradation modes driven by changing characteristics of a battery. The P2D model [28,29] stands out among these physics-based models due to its accuracy, capability to capture essential electrochemical phenomena, and flexibility to incorporate new physics [8,30]. The well-known P2D model utilises porous electrode theory to represent active particles as spheres across the electrode thickness. For a comprehensive understanding of the P2D model equations, refer to Brosa Planella et al. [27]. For the interest of this paper, we present a physics-based modelling framework for the composite electrode, SEI growth, mechanical stress, and cracking behaviour with governing equations focusing on the electro-chemo-mechanics.

The stress resulting from the (de)intercalation of lithium-ions within the active material is analogous to temperature-induced stress and is commonly referred to as diffusion-induced stress [9,10,31,32]. To mathematically describe this, Zhang et al. [10] developed a stress model for spherical electrode particles that considers the equilibrium of stresses in a free-standing spherical particle. Here, the model provides analytical solutions for radial stress
σ

, tangential stress
σ

, and displacement u . The equations governing stress for an elastic body, specifically in the context of spherical symmetry, can be expressed as follows:

The composite electrode model used in this study is adapted from Ai et al.’s work [33]. Here, only the main equations are provided for conciseness. It draws inspiration from experimental findings where silicon and graphite coexist as a two-phase composite [34]. The model employs separate spherical coordinate systems for each phase in the through-thickness direction, with distinct lithium-ion diffusion equations for silicon and graphite particles sharing the same surface potential. Two separate spherical particles are assumed for silicon and graphite at each Cartesian coordinate system node to describe stress behaviour in the individual phases of the composite electrode. The diffusion equation (Eq. (5)) and boundary conditions (Eqs. (6)) for the composite silicon and graphite negative electrode, considering stresses, are suitably modified with specific notations for silicon “Si” and graphite “Gr”.

). Eq. (5) is grounded in Fick’s second law of diffusion and encompasses diffusion-induced stress [35]. The diffusion-induced stress arises from compositional differences during lithium-ion (de)intercalation in host particles, involving radial, tangential, and hydrostatic stress, which then drives the particle fracture model. It impacts lithium-ion gradients within the electrode particles, and is thus, vital to incorporate into the model. Hydrostatic stress is determined using the radial and tangential constitutive stress–strain relationship, which is influenced by lithium-ion concentration. In this context, the surface area to volume ratios of silicon (
a

) and graphite (
a

) are represented by Eq. (7).

In their research, Chen et al. [34] determined the maximum lithium concentration in both silicon

. To normalise the expression in Eq. (8), the exchange current density of silicon is rescaled by
c

, considering silicon’s higher maximum lithium concentration compared to graphite. By combining the individual interfacial current densities per volume, the composite electrode current density
(

In this study, the open circuit potential (OCP) of silicon plays a crucial role in determining the reaction current density. Accurate modelling of the OCP is challenging due to voltage hysteresis resulting from the different phases formed during (de)lithiation [36]. The delithiation and lithiation OCP functions of silicon denoted as
ϕ
Si , ocp
de
and
ϕ
Si , ocp
li
respectively, are obtained using a sigmoid function (
sigmoid
( x )
=
[ 1 + sinh
( x )
]
/ 2
) for accounting for the voltage hysteresis, as shown in Eq. (11).
(11)
ϕ
Si , ocp
= sigmoid
−
100 I
Q
ϕ
Si , ocp
de
+ sigmoid
100 I
Q
ϕ
Si , ocp
li
where I is the applied current and Q is the cell capacity. The stress model presented in this study is integrated with the P2D model in PyBaMM to simulate the variation in stress distribution within the battery.

The electrode experiences substantial volume changes during cycling, causing mechanical stress and potential crack formation. At the end-of-life, significant fracture, primarily near the separator, can occur due to higher local reaction current density during cycling [9,37]. Electrode particle fracture contributes to degradation through three main mechanisms: an increased surface area for SEI growth, leading to further loss of lithium inventory; detachment of conductive additives during delithiation, causing reduced electronic conductivity; and complete fracture or detachment, resulting in electrical isolation of the particle and loss of active material [32,38,39].

The crack model utilised in this study is based on the fatigue crack model proposed by Deshpande et al. [40]. This study assumes micro-cracks on the electrode surface with fixed dimensions (width and crack density). Although the cracks grow in length during cycling, their width and density remain constant, with the fatigue crack growth model following Paris’ law [40].
(12)
d
l
cr
d N

k

t

σ

m
cr
;
σ
t
> 0

The parameter
t
0
represents the duration of a single cycle, while
b
cr
corresponds to the stress intensity factor correction. The constants
k
cr
and
m
cr
are determined based on experimental data [41]. The condition
σ
t
> 0
indicates that only tensile stress influences crack growth. Consequently, the rate of change of the crack area to volume ratio can be given.
(13)
d

During the repetitive cycling, LAM can occur due to electrochemical reactions between the electrodes and electrolyte, including positive electrode dissolution and mechanical damage resulting from stresses that cause particle cracking and island formation. Previous research has used maximum stress and strain energy as drivers of LAM, with higher C-rates correlating with increased stress and strain energy levels [11,42,43]. However, these methods rely on empirical parameters and require time-consuming parameter fitting due to experimental measurement challenges. In the current model, only LAM attributed to particle cracking is taken into account. This initiates a positive feedback loop by promoting particle fracture; as LAM reduces the interface area between the active material and electrolyte, it results in an elevated interfacial current density, leading to more significant concentration gradients and amplified mechanical stress. LAM, cracking, and SEI on cracks are interconnected, originating from the mechanical stress caused by concentration gradients. Both cracking and mechanical LAM stem from the same underlying physics; therefore, this work considers them in a coupled manner.

The core equations for LAM due to particle cracking are presented, with in-depth derivations available in [32,42]. The stress model in Eqs. (1) and (12) are used to estimate the decrease in the accessible volume fraction of active materials (
ɛ
a
).
(14)
∂
ɛ
a
∂ t
=
β
t
0
σ
h , max
−
σ
h , min
σ
c
m
2
σ
h , min
> 0 ,
where β and
m
2
are constants determined experimentally, and
σ
c
represents the critical stress. The critical stress for silicon is considered 0.72 GPa [44]. The subscripts ‘min’ and ‘max’ denote the minimum and maximum values, respectively.

In lithium-ion batteries, the organic electrolytes become unstable at voltages below 1 V vs Li/Li
+
. This instability leads to the electrolyte’s breakdown and its reaction with lithiated electrode particles, forming a SEI layer on the electrode surface. The SEI layer plays a role in battery degradation by trapping lithium, restricting its involvement in charge storage, and causing a loss of lithium inventory (LLI). Furthermore, the SEI layer hampers ion movement through the cell; increasing the impedance. Darling and Newman pioneered the incorporation of a solvent oxidation reaction into a lithium-ion battery model, offering insights into self-discharge processes and their impact on positive electrode SoC [45]. A mathematical model [46] predicting carbon electrode capacity loss during open-circuit voltage storage was presented, emphasising continuous reduction of organic solvent and lithium de-intercalation. A capacity-fade model was introduced, linking active lithium loss during cycling to continuous SEI film formation on the negative electrode [47]. A different method of understanding SEI growth involves considering the electron tunnelling process, where the rate-determining step is identified as the passage of electrons through the electrically insulated inner SEI [48]. The model proposed by Safari et al. [49] has identified two limitations in the primary SEI reaction: solvent molecule diffusion through the SEI and graphite’s role in reducing the solvent molecule. Their findings suggest that a diffusion-limited regime best aligns with experimental data. Consequently, in this work, the SEI growth model developed in PyBaMM, based on solvent diffusion limitation, is employed. The model assumes a steady state where the diffusion of solvent molecules through the SEI layer governs its growth, following Fick’s law.
(15)
N
sol
= −
D
sol
( T )
∂
c
sol
∂ l

D

( T )
represents the temperature-dependent solvent diffusion coefficient, l represents a specific location within the SEI layer, and
L
SEI
represents the thickness of the SEI layer (also corresponding to the outer surface location). Following this, the solution to this is:

( T )
L
SEI

The interfacial flux density in the SEI layer can be determined based on the principle of mass conservation.
(18)
N
SEI
= −
N
sol
=
c
sol , 0
D
sol
( T )
L
SEI
Thus, the rate at which the SEI layer thickness increases is:
(19)
∂
L
SEI
∂ t
= −
1
2
N
sol
V
̄
SEI
=
c
sol , 0
D
sol
( T )
V
̄
SEI
2
L
SEI
.

Here, the SEI layer is considered to have an Ohmic resistivity (
ρ
SEI
), resulting in a voltage drop,
η
SEI
, caused by the interfacial current density,
j
tot
.
(20)
η
SEI
=
ρ
SEI
L
SEI
j
tot
a
−

A simple approach for describing SEI on cracks is to apply the same SEI growth model on the cracks to account for the interaction between SEI growth and particle cracking [50]. However, it is important to consider the SEI formation on newly exposed crack surfaces is generally different from already formed surfaces. To simulate the SEI formation stage, a thin initial SEI layer
L
SEI , cr 0
is defined on the cracks, typically a fraction of the initial SEI layer thickness
L
SEI 0
. This allows for rapid growth of the initial SEI layer. As crack propagation leads to varying exposure times of the new surfaces to the electrolyte along the crack interface, the average thickness of the SEI layer on cracks
L
SEI , cr
is used for simplicity, with its time evolution determined accordingly.
(21)
∂
L
SEI , cr
∂ t
=
c
sol , 0
D
sol
( T )
V
̄
SEI
2
L
SEI , cr
+
∂
l
cr
∂ t
L
SEI , cr 0
−
L
SEI , cr
l
cr

Eq. (21) includes two terms describing different aspects of the SEI layer. The first term represents the diffusion-limited growth of the SEI on existing cracks, contributing positively to overall growth. The second term accounts for particle crack propagation, which increases the total surface area and leads to an effective decrease in the average thickness of the SEI layer on cracks, as denoted by its negative value.

The model in this work aims to integrate the distinct forms of degradation into a unified electrochemical–mechanical model. Here, the model accounts for the interactions between SEI growth, cracking, SEI on crack and LAM, as shown in Fig. 2. Within this model, the formation of SEI and the loss of lithium attributed to LAM constitute the primary drivers for LLI. The literature comprehensively describes graphite’s mechanical parameters [51,52]. However, due to the lack of available mechanical parameters for silicon in the existing literature, the LAM proportional term and cracking rate had to be estimated for the degradation analysis. These models have been incorporated as submodels in PyBaMM [20] and are accessible within PyBaMM’s framework, including the Single Particle Model, Single Particle Model with electrolyte, and Doyle-Fuller-Newman model (or P2D). Readers can refer to the open-source platform PyBaMM files of the composite electrode model on the PyBaMM GitHub public repository (https://github.com/pybamm-team/PyBaMM). The trends of the presented model have been validated through experimental degradation tests. The results presented in Section 4 demonstrate distinct stress-driven behaviour between the graphite and silicon phases.

In this section, model results on mechanical stress, loss of active materials in the silicon phase (LAM
S i
) and graphite phase (LAM
G r
) are presented to explore their interplay and impacts on degradation. The parameters are sourced from Ai et al. [33,53], based on a high-energy-density commercial battery (LG M50T). The LG M50T uses a SiO
x
-blended graphite negative electrode paired with an NMC811 cathode, offering a nominal energy of 18.2 Wh, and capacity of 5 Ah, as studied by Kirkaldy et al. [22]. For the sake of clarity and reproducibility, these parameter values are presented in Supplementary Tables 1 and 2. This work simulates different cyclic ageing tests to investigate stress generation and its impact on cell capacity degradation. These simulations are: (a) cycling the cell across the entire SoC range (0%–100%), equivalent to a total DoD, and (b) cycling the cell across a constrained low-SoC range (0%–30%), where we expected more significant silicon activity within the composite electrode. The high-SoC set (SoC 0%–100%) was designed to provoke all types of cell degradation. In contrast, we hypothesised that the low-SoC set of simulations would lead to accelerated silicon degradation. All the simulations were conducted at a constant temperature of 25 °C, with a 0.3C charging rate. These C-rates were based on the initial nominal capacity of 5 Ah at beginning-of-life (BoL), where 1C equated to 5 A. The upper (4.2 V) and lower voltage (2.5 V) cut-off limits remain constant across all cycles.

Simulations were conducted in PyBaMM to parameterise the presented model to replicate the experimental results observed in the experimental degradation tests. Figs. 3, S1 and S5 (Supplementary figures) show the simulated and experimental results. The relevant degradation mechanisms here include LLI resulting from the SEI layer growth and LAM due to mechanical degradation. The expansion-induced fracture of the particles exposes the active material to the electrolyte, leading to further SEI formation. This cyclic volume change and cracking during cycling contributes to the continuous consumption of lithium (LLI due to LAM).

Fig. 3 illustrates the behaviour of the loss of active material in the negative electrode (LAM
N e
), LAM
S i
and LAM
G r
, which increases during cycling over 0-100% (Figs. 3 (a)–(c)) and 0%–30% (Figs. 3 (d)–(e)) SoC cycling ranges. The first notable observation is the dominance of the LAM
N e
( ∼ 21% at 9.5 kAh charge throughput) compared to loss of active material in the positive electrode (LAM
P e
, ∼ 6.7% at 9.5 kAh charge throughput), as shown in Figure S1, for cells cycled over the 0%–100% SoC range. Fig. 3 (e) shows that with the 0%–30% SoC range, the LAM
S i
approximately doubles compared to the 0%–100% SoC case. This is because the silicon is more electrochemically active in the low SoC range due to its different OCP profile to graphite. However, overtime, the increase in LAM
S i
slows down.

To understand why the LAM trends follow this behaviour, the stress profiles in the individual phases over the lifetime of use, were analysed. Here, Fig. 4 (a) shows how the average volumetric interfacial current density changes for silicon and graphite at the beginning-of-life (BoL) and end-of-life (EoL). The EoL state is characterised by a 10 kAh charge throughput from the BoL condition. Notable changes include the delayed electrochemical activity, in the silicon phase, to lower SoC ranges with aging, due to the increase in LAM
S i
. For the 0%–100% SoC cycling, the magnitude of the stress in the silicon, decreases (Fig. 4 (b)), whereas for graphite, it increases (Fig. 4 (c)), even as LAM
G r
increases. Here, readers are directed to Figure S2 for details on the stress profiles at BoL and EoL for silicon and graphite. The cause of this shift is due, in part, to the negative electrode OCP curve change resulting from accelerated silicon capacity degradation during ageing. Here, an increasing proportion of the silicon within the composite electrode becomes inaccessible due to the cell voltage cut-off occurring earlier during ageing; explaining the observed slowdown in LAM
S i
increase.

Here, we see in Fig. 4 (b) that silicon’s average stress, and variation over a cycle, decreases during ageing while graphite’s average increases. This increase in graphite stress is a response to the reduction in silicon’s electrochemical activity due to LAM
S i
. Further exploration of the stress distribution across the electrode is provided in Figures S7 and S8, for interested readers. By examining the first and last cycles in Figure S9, we establish how degradation affects the concentration gradient range of both materials.

Furthermore, the model reveals intriguing interactions between the graphite and silicon phases. Specifically, for cells cycled between 0%–30% SoC, where the silicon is more electrochemically active, the silicon current density drops below zero at the start of discharge (after a charge) (Fig. 4(d)). The initial negative silicon current density indicates that the graphite is intercalating the silicon even when the cell is in a discharge mode. This interesting behaviour arises due to the hysteresis in the silicon OCP profile compared to graphite’s and their shared potential in the anode.

In Fig. 4 (e) and (f), the stress profile evolution of the two phases for 0%–30% SoC cycling are presented. It shows that graphite tangential stresses gradually stabilise, with the graphite experiencing higher stresses at BoL when operating within the 0%–30% SoC range, in contrast to the 0-100 SoC range. Additionally, when silicon becomes inactive in the 0%–30% SoC cells, the stress in graphite rises since graphite is now cycled exclusively, as observed in Figure S8.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of C-rate and silicon content
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. 5 and 6 extend our previous work [54] and delve into the impact of ageing-induced changes in stress generation within the silicon/graphite phases across different C-rates and silicon content. Figs. 5 (a)–(b) and 6 (a)–(b) depict the average surface tangential stress for graphite and silicon at both BoL and EoL for two different C-rates. Here, lower stress levels at lower C-rates are observed for the graphite phase, whilst higher stresses at low C-rates are seen in silicon. This seemingly counter-intuitive behaviour in the silicon is due to the increased silicon utilisation, as the lower C-rates enables more accessible capacity at high DoDs; the region where silicon is more electrochemically active. The stress distribution within the composite electrode during discharge is illustrated in Figures S7 and S8 for further elaboration. Here, silicon exhibits a near uniform through-plane stress distribution that homogenises further at higher DoD.

The impact of varying silicon (Si) content on degradation and capacity fade was also be explored with the model. Chen et al. [34] reported that the LG M50T cell had a silicon loading of 10 wt%, determined through surface images of the composite negative electrode using energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy. Here, we compared electrode performance with 10 wt% and 7 wt% Si while keeping binder and carbon additive quantities consistent. The influence of Si content on discharge capacity is illustrated in Fig. 5 (c), showing that adding Si to composite electrodes enhances discharge capacity, as expected. Here for the 7 wt% Si electrodes, the Si current density increased due to less surface area than the 10 wt% Si (Fig. 5 (d)), resulting in a more significant LAM
S i
, as depicted in Fig. 5 (e).

Within the 0%–30% SoC range, both 10 wt% and 7 wt% Si-containing cells experience similar losses of active material at the EoL, as shown in Fig. 6 (f). The 10 wt% Si cell has a lower fade rate compared to the 7 wt% Si cell up to 5 kAh of charge throughput. Subsequently, these curves overlap, with both exhibiting approximately 80% LAM
S i
. It may be concluded that the variation of Si% in the lower SoC range affects the initial degradation rate, but the total LAM
S i
converges to similar values at the EoL.

Returning to the observed negative current densities behaviour in the silicon, Fig. 6 (e) shows this occurring between 70 to ∼ 85% DoD range at the BoL, with this effect more significant with the higher Si loading, but ultimately dissipating by the EoL

To help answer the question ‘is silicon worth it?’ the effect of the rapid loss of silicon on the cell capacity is shown in Fig. 7 alongside the capacity estimated for the same cell if additional graphite had been used to achieve the capacity of the cell instead of silicon. It can be seen that the usable capacity of the cell starts dropping below the capacity of the graphite-only cell, in this case, after a charge throughput of 7 kAh (Fig. 7). As discussed above, as the silicon/graphite composite cell degrades, the silicon degrades faster than the graphite, with the utilisation of the remaining silicon decreasing with ageing as the voltage cut-offs are reached early. If performance at EoL was the most important, it would have been better off to have had more graphite in the cell at the beginning of life than silicon. To summarise, for this generation of silicon composites, ‘if you use it, you lose it’.

However, this does not necessarily mean that silicon is not worth it and poses the addition question of, does the silicon have value if unused? The silicon can have value if it enables higher utilisation of the graphite without fear of range anxiety. In essence, silicon can be used to provide low SoC capacity, enabling more optimised performance in the higher (and more used) SoC range. This can be beneficial in applications where full depth of discharge is a rare occurrence, which is usually true for EVs. In Fig. 7 the useable capacity of the cell with silicon/graphite composite electrode cycled with C-rates of 0.3C and 1C for charge and discharge, where 9/10 cycles are for 30%–100% SoC and 1/10 cycles are for 0%–100% SoC is shown, highlighting the useable capacity remains above the line for a graphite only cell for much longer.

Therefore, the answer to the question ’Is silicon worth it?’, can be yes or no, but depends heavily on design and usage profile. Here, we highlight the value of the open-source modelling framework that we have created in PyBaMM, which can be used to provide guidance on this question for different use cases. As commercial cells incorporating improved silicon and electrolyte become commercially available, the answer to the question for different applications may change. If the physics of degradation for the new materials remains the same, i.e. crack-induced accelerated SEI layer growth, then this model can be re-used, simply requiring reparameterisation. However, if the new materials degrade in fundamentally different ways, then those degradation mechanisms will need to be studied further before implementing in the model as upgrades.

While the model reasonably agrees with experiments, some limitations should be acknowledged. Here, simplifying assumptions have been made that may limit the model’s accuracy under certain conditions. For instance, the stress model assumes spherical, free-standing particles, but in reality, particles have irregular shapes and are surrounded by binder and conductive additives which may influence the exact stress profile and fracture behaviour.

Additional interacting degradation modes like electrolyte dry-out may also need to be incorporated. In the cathode, the NMC layered oxide structure can undergo irreversible phase transitions, which deteriorates capacity retention and voltage performance over cycling. Additionally, transition metals can dissolve into the electrolyte, further exacerbating capacity fade; effects which have yet to be incorporated.

Overall, while providing valuable insights into composite electrode degradation trends, there are some limitations stemming from the simplified assumptions and a lack of available parameters, measured in-situ, over a broader range of conditions. Future work should incorporate additional physics, like distinct graphite volume changes under staging, and expand the experimental validation space.

Composite silicon/graphite battery anodes are likely to be increasingly deployed due to their relatively high specific capacity, and thus understanding their degradation behaviour is critical. This paper presents new insights into the coupled electro-chemo-mechanics which drive the complex degradation processes in these composite systems. The volume changes occurring during lithium-ion (de)intercalation in the active materials can lead to crack propagation, exposing fresh active material surfaces to the electrolyte, resulting in further electrolyte reduction and subsequent SEI layer formation and growth. We describe the physics of this process with an electrochemical–mechanical model which decouples the reactions/stresses in the individual silicon/graphite phases. Using PyBaMM models, we explore how diffusion-induced stress drives the evolution of degradation modes over the lifetime of a battery under different operational conditions and designs (C-rate, SoC range and silicon loading). The P2D-based model, integrated with models for SEI growth, stress generation, cracking and LAM, is validated using experimental data from degradation tests conducted on LG M50T cells.

Key insights include the description of the self-limiting LAM
S i
with cycling, which we find to be caused by the decreasing stress levels in the silicon resulting from its reduced utilisation with cycling. Here, graphite provides the majority of the reaction current density at high SoCs; with silicon only becoming active at low SoCs due to its OCP profile. As the cell ages, higher DoD is needed for the silicon reaction current density to become appreciable. Furthermore, we find that the LAM
S i
decreases as the C-rate and silicon loading is increased. In the first instance, the high C-rate behaviour is due to voltage cut-offs being reached before silicon becomes fully utilised. In the latter case, the higher silicon loading, distributes the reaction current density over more active material, resulting in lower concentration gradients and thus stress.

Finally, we highlight the highly detrimental operating mode of low SoC cycling, where silicon is most active. Here we show that the LAM
S i
can be approximately doubled when comparing 100% SoC cycling vs 30%–0% due to silicon’s increased activity. Compounding this, we also find that the silicon hysteresis effect can cause one phase to charge the other, especially in the low SoC regions; leading to increased current density levels.

Silicon’s high capacity makes it appealing for inclusion in composite electrodes, but it degrades faster than graphite. Due to its multi-component nature, the composite electrodes display non-uniform behaviour during ageing. Avoiding low SoC levels, lower C-rates and increasing the silicon content emerge as potential strategies to reduce silicon phase degradation. Additionally, the intriguing observation that higher silicon percentages might contribute to reduced degradation rates underscores the importance of optimising active material ratios to balance performance and longevity. The findings suggest that the extra capacity provided by silicon becomes more meaningful if the low SoC range is utilised, highlighting its potential as a backup reserve for real-life scenarios where users tend to avoid the 0%–20% range. These considerations contribute to a more nuanced evaluation of whether incorporating silicon is “worth it” in composite electrodes. In conclusion, this research provides valuable insights into the intricate interplay between silicon and graphite in composite electrodes. While incorporating silicon poses challenges, it appears “worth it” when used in moderation within composite electrodes.

Mayur P. Bonkile: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yang Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Niall Kirkaldy: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Valentin Sulzer: Writing – review & editing, Software. Robert Timms: Writing – review & editing, Software. Huizhi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Gregory Offer: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Billy Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was kindly supported by the EPSRC Faraday Institution Multi-Scale Modelling project (EP/S003053/1, grant number FIRG003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2024.234256.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
MMC S1
Supplementary Material.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A reversible graphite-lithium negative electrode for electrochemical generators

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Yazami and P. Touzain, “A reversible graphite-lithium negative electrode for electrochemical generators,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 9, no. 3, pp. 365–371, 1983.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural changes in silicon anodes during lithium insertion/extraction

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. N. Obrovac and L. Christensen, “Structural changes in silicon anodes during lithium insertion/extraction,” Electrochemical and Solid-State Letters, vol. 7, p. A93, mar 2004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silicon oxides: A promising family of anode materials for lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Liu, Q. Yu, Y. Zhao, R. He, M. Xu, S. Feng, S. Li, L. Zhou, and L. Mai, “Silicon oxides: a promising family of anode materials for lithium-ion batteries,” Chem. Soc. Rev., vol. 48, pp. 285–309, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modeling and simulation of inhomogeneities in a 18650 nickel-rich, silicon-graphite lithium-ion cell during fast charging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Sturm, A. Rheinfeld, I. Zilberman, F. Spingler, S. Kosch, F. Frie, and A. Jossen, “Modeling and simulation of inhomogeneities in a 18650 nickel-rich, silicon-graphite lithium-ion cell during fast charging,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 412, pp. 204–223, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanistic investigation of silicon-graphite/LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 commercial cells for non-intrusive diagnosis and prognosis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Anseán, G. Baure, M. González, I. Cameán, A. García, and M. Dubarry, “Mechanistic investigation of silicon-graphite/lini0.8mn0.1co0.1o2 commercial cells for non-intrusive diagnosis and prognosis,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 459, p. 227882, 2020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of half-cell open-circuit potential curve of silicon-graphite in a physics-based model for lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Gao, Z. Sun, D. Zhang, D. Shi, and X. Zhang, “Determination of half-cell open-circuit potential curve of silicon-graphite in a physics-based model for lithium-ion batteries,” Applied Energy, vol. 349, p. 121621, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation diagnostics for lithium ion cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. R. Birkl, M. R. Roberts, E. McTurk, P. G. Bruce, and D. A. Howey, “Degradation diagnostics for lithium ion cells,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 341, pp. 373–386, 2017.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium ion battery degradation: What you need to know

Marzook, K. N. Radhakrishnan, H. Wang, Y. Patel, B. Wu, and G. J. Offer, “Lithium ion battery degradation: what you need to know,” Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys., vol. 23, pp. 8200–8221, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical thermal-mechanical modelling of stress inhomogeneity in lithium-ion pouch cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Ai, L. Kraft, J. Sturm, A. Jossen, and B. Wu, “Electrochemical thermal-mechanical modelling of stress inhomogeneity in lithium-ion pouch cells,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 167, p. 013512, oct 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-ion battery degradation: Measuring rapid loss of active silicon in silicon–graphite composite electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Kirkaldy, M. A. Samieian, G. J. Offer, M. Marinescu, and Y. Patel, “Lithium-ion battery degradation: Measuring rapid loss of active silicon in silicon–graphite composite electrodes,” ACS Applied Energy Materials, vol. 5, no. 11, pp. 13367–13376, 2022.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Lai, Y. Zheng, and T. Sun, “A comparative study of different equivalent circuit models for estimating state-of-charge of lithium-ion batteries,” Electrochimica Acta, vol. 259, pp. 566–577, 2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A continuum of physics-based lithium-ion battery models reviewed

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. B. Planella, W. Ai, A. M. Boyce, A. Ghosh, I. Korotkin, S. Sahu, V. Sulzer, R. Timms, T. G. Tranter, M. Zyskin, S. J. Cooper, J. S. Edge, J. M. Foster, M. Marinescu, B. Wu, and G. Richardson, “A continuum of physics-based lithium-ion battery models reviewed,” Progress in Energy, vol. 4, p. 042003, jul 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mathematical modeling of secondary lithium batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. G. Botte, V. R. Subramanian, and R. E. White, “Mathematical modeling of secondary lithium batteries,” Electrochimica Acta, vol. 45, no. 15, pp. 2595–2609, 2000.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of models for predicting the cycling performance of lithium ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Santhanagopalan, Q. Guo, P. Ramadass, and R. E. White, “Review of models for predicting the cycling performance of lithium ion batteries,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 156, no. 2, pp. 620–628, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Differential thermal voltammetry for tracking of degradation in lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wu, V. Yufit, Y. Merla, R. F. Martinez-Botas, N. P. Brandon, and G. J. Offer, “Differential thermal voltammetry for tracking of degradation in lithium-ion batteries,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 273, pp. 495–501, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modeling diffusion-induced stress in Li-ion cells with porous electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Christensen, “Modeling diffusion-induced stress in li-ion cells with porous electrodes,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 157, p. A366, feb 2010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modeling mechanical degradation in lithium ion batteries during cycling: Solid electrolyte interphase fracture

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Laresgoiti, S. Käbitz, M. Ecker, and D. U. Sauer, “Modeling mechanical degradation in lithium ion batteries during cycling: Solid electrolyte interphase fracture,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 300, pp. 112–122, 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A composite electrode model for lithium-ion batteries with silicon/graphite negative electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Ai, N. Kirkaldy, Y. Jiang, G. Offer, H. Wang, and B. Wu, “A composite electrode model for lithium-ion batteries with silicon/graphite negative electrodes,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 527, p. 231142, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of experimental techniques for parameterization of multi-scale lithium-ion battery models

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.-H. Chen, F. B. Planella, K. O’Regan, D. Gastol, W. D. Widanage, and E. Kendrick, “Development of experimental techniques for parameterization of multi-scale lithium-ion battery models,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 167, p. 080534, jan 2020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evolution of stress within a spherical insertion electrode particle under potentiostatic and galvanostatic operation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-T. Cheng and M. W. Verbrugge, “Evolution of stress within a spherical insertion electrode particle under potentiostatic and galvanostatic operation,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 190, no. 2, pp. 453–460, 2009.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltage hysteresis model for silicon electrodes for lithium ion batteries, including multi-step phase transformations, crystallization and amorphization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Jiang, G. Offer, J. Jiang, M. Marinescu, and H. Wang, “Voltage hysteresis model for silicon electrodes for lithium ion batteries, including multi-step phase transformations, crystallization and amorphization,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 167, p. 130533, oct 2020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heterogeneous damage in Li-ion batteries: Experimental analysis and theoretical modeling

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Xu, Y. Yang, F. Yin, P. Liu, P. Cloetens, Y. Liu, F. Lin, and K. Zhao, “Heterogeneous damage in li-ion batteries: Experimental analysis and theoretical modeling,” Journal of the Mechanics and Physics of Solids, vol. 129, pp. 160–183, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Life simulation of a graphite/LiFePO4 cell under cycling and storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Delacourt and M. Safari, “Life simulation of a graphite/lifepo4 cell under cycling and storage,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 159, p. A1283, jul 2012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A single particle model with chemical/mechanical degradation physics for lithium ion battery state of health (SOH) estimation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, K. Adewuyi, N. Lotfi, R. Landers, and J. Park, “A single particle model with chemical/mechanical degradation physics for lithium ion battery state of health (soh) estimation,” Applied Energy, vol. 212, pp. 1178–1190, 2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Battery cycle life prediction with coupled chemical degradation and fatigue mechanics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Deshpande, M. Verbrugge, Y.-T. Cheng, J. Wang, and P. Liu, “Battery cycle life prediction with coupled chemical degradation and fatigue mechanics,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 159, no. 10, pp. A1730–A1738, 2012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation of lithium ion batteries employing graphite negatives and nickel–cobalt–manganese oxide + spinel manganese oxide positives: Part 2, chemical–mechanical degradation model

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Purewal, J. Wang, J. Graetz, S. Soukiazian, H. Tataria, and M. W. Verbrugge, “Degradation of lithium ion batteries employing graphite negatives and nickel–cobalt–manganese oxide spinel manganese oxide positives: Part 2, chemical–mechanical degradation model,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 272, pp. 1154–1161, 2014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review and performance comparison of mechanical-chemical degradation models for lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. M. Reniers, G. Mulder, and D. A. Howey, “Review and performance comparison of mechanical–chemical degradation models for lithium-ion batteries,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 166, no. 14, pp. A3189–A3200, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A coupled electrochemical-thermal-mechanical degradation modelling approach for lifetime assessment of lithium-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. chun Yang, Y. Hua, D. Qiao, Y. bo Lian, Y. wei Pan, and Y. ling He, “A coupled electrochemical-thermal-mechanical degradation modelling approach for lifetime assessment of lithium-ion batteries,” Electrochimica Acta, vol. 326, p. 134928, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantitative fracture strength and plasticity measurements of lithiated silicon nanowires by in situ TEM tensile experiments

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kushima, J. Y. Huang, and J. Li, “Quantitative fracture strength and plasticity measurements of lithiated silicon nanowires by in situ tem tensile experiments.,” ACS nano, vol. 6 11, pp. 9425–32, 2012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modeling side reactions in composite liy Mn2O4 electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Darling and J. Newman, “Modeling side reactions in composite liy mn2o4 electrodes,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 145, p. 990, mar 1998.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simulation of capacity loss in carbon electrode for lithium-ion cells during storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. P. Ramasamy, J.-W. Lee, and B. N. Popov, “Simulation of capacity loss in carbon electrode for lithium-ion cells during storage,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 166, no. 1, pp. 266–272, 2007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of first principles capacity fade model for Li-ion cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Ramadass, B. Haran, P. M. Gomadam, R. White, and B. N. Popov, “Development of first principles capacity fade model for li-ion cells,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 151, p. A196, jan 2004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Connecting the irreversible capacity loss in Li-ion batteries with the electronic insulating properties of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) components

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.-X. Lin, Z. Liu, K. Leung, L.-Q. Chen, P. Lu, and Y. Qi, “Connecting the irreversible capacity loss in li-ion batteries with the electronic insulating properties of solid electrolyte interphase (sei) components,” Journal of Power Sources, vol. 309, pp. 221–230, 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multimodal physics-based aging model for life prediction of Li-ion batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Safari, M. Morcrette, A. Teyssot, and C. Delacourt, “Multimodal physics-based aging model for life prediction of li-ion batteries,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 156, p. A145, dec 2008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-ion battery degradation: How to model it

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. E. J. O’Kane, W. Ai, G. Madabattula, D. Alonso-Alvarez, R. Timms, V. Sulzer, J. S. Edge, B. Wu, G. J. Offer, and M. Marinescu, “Lithium-ion battery degradation: how to model it,” Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys., vol. 24, pp. 7909–7922, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stress generation and fracture in lithium insertion materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Christensen and J. Newman, “Stress generation and fracture in lithium insertion materials,” Journal of Solid State Electrochemistry, vol. 10, no. 5, pp. 293–319, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A mathematical model of stress generation and fracture in lithium manganese oxide

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Christensen and J. Newman, “A mathematical model of stress generation and fracture in lithium manganese oxide,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 153, p. A1019, apr 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical thermal-mechanical modelling of stress inhomogeneity in lithium-ion pouch cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Ai, L. Kraft, J. Sturm, A. Jossen, and B. Wu, “Electrochemical thermal-mechanical modelling of stress inhomogeneity in lithium-ion pouch cells,” Journal of The Electrochemical Society, vol. 167, no. 1, p. 013512, 2020.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coupled electrochemical-thermal-mechanical stress modelling in composite silicon/graphite lithium-ion battery electrodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. P. Bonkile, Y. Jiang, N. Kirkaldy, V. Sulzer, R. Timms, H. Wang, G. Offer, and B. Wu, “Coupled electrochemical-thermal-mechanical stress modelling in composite silicon/graphite lithium-ion battery electrodes,” Journal of Energy Storage, vol. 73, p. 108609, 2023.
